petitioner_background: The petitioner sought to classify the beneficiary as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act as an alien of extraordinary ability. No specific information about the beneficiary's field of work, nationality, or career qualifications is provided in the decision text, as the appeal was summarily dismissed on procedural grounds without reaching the merits of the extraordinary ability claim.
outcome_reasoning: The appeal was summarily dismissed on procedural grounds. Counsel requested 150 days to submit a brief and additional evidence, but neither the petitioner nor counsel identified any particular item that would take 150 days to procure or explained why it would take so long. Approximately seven months after the appeal was filed on June 15, 2004, the AAO had received nothing further. The AAO found that a mere statement that good cause exists to allow 150 days to supplement the record is insufficient, and because counsel failed to specifically address the reasons stated for denial and provided no additional evidence, the appeal was summarily dismissed pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(v).
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The appeal was summarily dismissed on procedural grounds. Counsel requested 150 days to submit a brief and/or evidence, claiming good cause based on the volume of evidence criticized by the director, but failed to identify any specific item that would require such an extended timeframe. Seven months later, no brief or additional evidence had been submitted. Counsel failed to specifically identify any erroneous conclusion of law or statement of fact as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(v), warranting summary dismissal.
